title: Preparation and Properties of Carboxylic Acids

concepts.title: Nomenclature of carboxylic acids
concepts.conceptBody:
Nomenclature

The aliphatic carboxylic acids are commonly known by their initial names.

Example {Common names}

  • $\text{HCOOH}$ Formic acid
  • $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{s}}\text{COOH}$ Acetic acid
  • $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{s}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}$ Propionic acid

IUPAC naming: These are normally suffixed as “oic acid”, when $\text{COOH}$ is the principal functional group and its ca\rbon is counted while numbering longest chain. The carboxyl carbon is assigned number 1 in the IUPAC nomenclature. For example,

1. 3, 4,-dimethyl pentanoic acid

2. HCOOH Methanoic acid

3. 4.

Dicarboxylic acids are named as “alkanedioic acids”. For example, is named as ethanedioic acid.

Naming Acid Chlorides & Anhydrides

Acids chlorides are named systematically as acyl chlorides.

An acid anhydride is named by substituting anhydride for acid in the name of acid from which it is derived.

If two different alkyl groups are present, then alkanoic are named in alphabetical order

Naming Amides and Imides: The names of amides are formed by replacing - oic acid by amide or - carboxylic acid by carboxamide.

 

If the nitrogen atom of amide has any alkyl groups as substituents, the name of the amide is prefixed by the capital letter$\text{N}$ i to indicate substitution on nitrogen, followed by name(s) of alkyl group(s).

concepts.title: Physical Properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Physical Properties

(a) Physical state and smell 

The first three members are colourless liquids and have pungent smell. The next six members are oily liquids with a faint unpleasant odour.

Still higher acids are colourless waxy solids.

Benzoic acids and its homologues are colourless solids.

(b) Boiling points : They have higher boiling points than the corresponding alcohols of comparable molecular masses.

Carboxylic acids have higher boiling points due to the presence of intramolecular hydrogen bonding. Due to the hydrogen bonding, carboxylic acids exists as dimers.

(c) Melting point : In the case of first ten carboxylic acids, the melting points of acids containing even number of carbon atoms is higher than the next lower and higher member containing odd number of carbon atoms.

The melting and boiling points of aromatic acids are usually higher than those of aliphatic acids of comparable molecular masses.

(d) Solubility in water : The first four members of aliphatic carboxylic acids are very soluble in water. The solubility in water decreases gradually with rise in molecular mass. All are soluble in alcohol or ether.

Benzoic acid is sparingly soluble in cold water but is soluble in hot water, alcohol and ether.

concepts.title: Acidity of Carboxylic acids
concepts.conceptBody:
Acidity of Carboxylic Acids

The acidity of a carboxylic acid is due to the resonance stabilization of its anion.

The acidity of carboxylic acid depends very much on the substituent attached to $-\text{COOH}$ group. Since acidity is due to the resonance stabilization of anion, substituents causing stabilization of anion increases acidity whereas substituent causing destabilization of anion decreases acidity. For example, electron withdrawing group disperses the negative charge of the anion and hence makes it more stable causing the increase in the acidity of the corresponding acid. On the other hand, electron releasing group increases the negative charge on the anion and hence makes it less stable causing the decrease in acidity.

  • Increase in the number of halogen atoms on $\alpha $-position increases the acidity, e.g.

$\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\text{COOH}>\text{CHC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\text{COOH}>\text{ClC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}\text{. }$

  • Increase in the distance of halogen from$\text{COOH}$ decrease the acidity, e.g.

concepts.title: Oxidation of Alcohol by Kmno4
concepts.conceptBody:
General Methods of Preparation

1. By oxidation of primary alcohols or aldehydes oxidation of primary alcohols can be achieved by acidified ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}$ or acidified/alkaline $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

\(\mathrm{RCH}_2 \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow [\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}]{(i) \mathrm{KMnO}_4 / \mathrm{OH}^{-}}\mathrm{RCOOH}\)

Aldehydes can be oxidized to carboxylic acids with mild oxidizing agents such as

$\text{Ag}\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)_{2}^{+}\overline{\text{O}}H$

$\text{R}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow[\left( ii \right){{H}_{3}}{{O}^{+}}]{\text{(i) A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{O or Ag}\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)_{2}^{+}}\text{R}-\text{COOH}$

concepts.title: By Oxidation of Alkenes and by Holoform
concepts.conceptBody:
By oxidation of alkenes when alkenes are treated with an oxidizing agent such as $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ or on oxidative ozonlysis, it forms carboxylic acids.

$\text{RCH}=\text{CH}{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) Hea}{{\text{t}}_{2}},{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}]{\text{(i) KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}}\text{RCOOH}+{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\text{COOH}$

$\text{RCH}=\text{CH}{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\xrightarrow[\overset{\text{ }}{\mathop{\text{ (ii) }{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O, distill }}}\,]{\text{(i) }{{\text{O}}_{3}}}\text{RCOOH}+{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\text{COOH}$

By haloform reaction

  • This method involves oxidation of methyl ketones with hypohalite ion,$\text{X}{{\text{O}}^{\ominus }}\left( \text{NaOH}+{{\text{X}}_{2}} \right)$

Note: In this method, the acid produced has one $\text{C}$ atom less than parent ketone.

concepts.title: By oxidation of alkyl benzene
concepts.conceptBody:
By oxidation of alkyl benzene

When the arenes containing an $\alpha$-hydrogen atom are reacted with a strong oxidizing agent like alkaline $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, it forms benzoic acid.

concepts.title: Formation and Hydrolysis of Nitriles
concepts.conceptBody:
Formation and Hydrolysis of Nitriles

Nitriles can be hydrolyzed by dilute acid to generate carboxylic acids.

$\text{ E}\text{.g}\text{. }\quad \text{R}-\text{C}\equiv \text{N}\frac{{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}}{\text{or}{{\text{ }}^{-}}\text{OH}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}$

Nitriles are easily made by the action of cyanide ion as a nucleophile on alkyl halides (or tosylates).

$\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{Br}\xrightarrow{\text{NaCN}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}\equiv \text{N}\xrightarrow{{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}$

Again the overall transformation is from alkyl halide to a carboxylic acid with an extra carbon atom .

concepts.title: Using Grignard reagent and Koch reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Using Grignard Reagent

Grignard Reagents react with carbon dioxide to yield magnesium carboxylate acidification produces carboxylic acids.

Note: The acid thus formed has one carbon more than the alkyl group of the Grignard reagent.

By hydrolysis of 1,1,1-Trihalogen Derivatives

By alkenes (Koch Reaction)

A recent method for manufacturing fatty acids is to heat an olefin with carbon monoxide and steam under pressure at $300-400\text{C}$ in the pressure of catalyst. e.g. phosphoric acid.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+\text{CO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow[\text{steam }300-{{400}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C}]{{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{COOH}$

Decarboxylation of gemdicarboxylic acid

Monocarboxylic acids may also result from the decarboxylation of dicarboxylic acids (when the two$\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}$ groups are present on same carbon). Acetic acid is thus obtained by heating/ warming malonic acid.

concepts.title: By hydrolysis of Acid Derivative
concepts.conceptBody:
Hydrolysis of acid derivatives

Another useful method constitutes the hydrolysis of carboxylic acid derivatives such as acyl halides, acid amides, acid anhydrides or esters.
